title: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
summary: Two networks trained adversarially: Generator creates fake data, Discriminator distinguishes real from fake. The competition drives both to improve. Training is notoriously unstable (mode collapse, oscillation). Architectural innovations (DCGAN, StyleGAN, Wasserstein GAN) address stability.
key_insights: Minimax game: Generator minimizes, Discriminator maximizes V(D,G) = E[log D(x)] + E[log(1-D(G(z)))]
Generator never sees real data directly — learns only through discriminator's feedback
Mode collapse: generator produces limited variety (finds one thing that fools discriminator and repeats it)
Wasserstein GAN: replace JS divergence with Earth Mover distance — smoother gradients, more stable training
GANs produce very sharp outputs (vs VAEs which tend to be blurry) because adversarial loss penalizes unrealism

mathematical_core.objective: min_G max_D V(D,G) = E_x[log D(x)] + E_z[log(1-D(G(z)))]
mathematical_core.generator: G: z → x̂, maps random noise z ~ N(0,I) to synthetic data
mathematical_core.discriminator: D: x → [0,1], probability that input is real
mathematical_core.nash_equilibrium: Optimal: D(x) = 0.5 for all x (can't tell real from fake), G distribution matches real data

related: DIFFUSION.BASIC — modern replacement for many GAN applications
